day3_part1:
第三天：产品市场契合度的层级

Day 3: Levels of Product-Market Fit

Product-Market Fit isn't a black box — a new framework to help B2B founders find it, faster

产品市场契合度并非黑箱——一个全新的框架，帮助B2B创始人更快地找到它

4.8.2024

2024年4月8日

Most people describe finding product-market fit as an art, not a science. But when it comes to sales-led B2B startups, we've reverse engineered a method to increase the odds of unlocking it.

大多数人将找到产品市场契合度描述为一门艺术，而非科学。但对于以销售为主导的B2B初创公司来说，我们已经逆向工程出一种方法，以提高解锁产品市场契合度的几率。

We've worked with some of the world's most iconic enterprise founders and distilled what they did in their first six months into a series of tactical sessions for taking a straighter path to PMF. Today, we're open sourcing the first session, which shares how we've broken PMF down to an incredibly granular level in order to help more builders get there faster.

我们与世界上一些最具标志性的企业创始人合作，并将他们在前六个月所做的工作提炼成一系列战术课程，以帮助创业者走上通往PMF的更直接道路。今天，我们开源第一节课程，分享我们是如何将PMF分解到极其细致的层面，以帮助更多建设者更快地达到目标。

Even though finding product-market fit is the single most important objective any company must achieve, it's still a black box for founders — seemingly requiring some mysterious combination of luck, market timing, and sheer grit.

尽管找到产品市场契合度是任何公司必须实现的最重要的单一目标，但对于创始人来说，它仍然是一个黑箱——似乎需要运气、市场时机和纯粹毅力的某种神秘组合。

While much has been written on the topic, no one has broken down the inputs, leading indicators and tactical steps that could increase the odds of finding PMF. In our view, one of the key reasons this topic hasn't been successfully 'framework-ified' is that most folks still talk about the concept in generalities.

虽然关于这个话题已经有很多著述，但没有人分解过可以提高找到PMF几率的投入、领先指标和战术步骤。在我们看来，这个话题未能成功框架化的关键原因之一是，大多数人仍然以笼统的方式谈论这个概念。

Fish are jumping into the boat. Leads are falling on the floor. You're chasing — not pushing — a boulder. The product is being pulled out of your hands. You'll know it when you see it. You can feel when it's not happening. These descriptions lack the specificity that we see in other areas of company building.

鱼儿跳进船里。潜在客户自己送上门来。你在追逐——而不是推动——一块巨石。产品正从你手中被拉走。当你看到它时你就会知道。当它没有发生时你能感觉到。这些描述缺乏我们在公司建设的其他领域所看到的具体性。

Another issue? Most resources target a wide range of builders — which results in watered-down advice that's more hand-wavy than tactical. Founders are left with approaches that are neither replicable nor practical, ranging from unhelpful 'We kind of just started going viral' founding stories to one-size-fits-all frameworks that don't address the nuances of their specific business.

另一个问题？大多数资源针对的是广泛的创业者——这导致建议被稀释，更像是挥手示意而非战术指导。创始人得到的方法既不可复制也不实用，从无用的我们差不多就是开始走红了这样的创业故事到不能解决其特定业务细微差别的一刀切框架。

While the hunt for PMF can be more of a dice game with poor odds for, say, consumer apps, our experience has taught us that for sales-led B2B companies, you can reduce the role of luck.

虽然对于消费类应用来说，寻找PMF更像是一场胜算不大的掷骰子游戏，但我们的经验告诉我们，对于以销售为主导的B2B公司，你可以减少运气的作用。

Here's how we've developed that conviction: As a firm of former founders and startup builders, we're coming up on our 20th year of investing in pre-product-market fit startups. Along with Square, Notion, Looker, and Verkada, we've backed hundreds of companies when they were just a couple of founders and an idea.

以下是我们如何形成这一信念的：作为一家由前创始人和初创企业建设者组成的公司，我们即将迎来投资产品市场契合前初创公司的第20个年头。除了Square、Notion、Looker和Verkada之外，当许多公司还只有几个创始人和一个想法时，我们就投资了它们。

Drawing on our own data and observations from more than 500 investments, we've spent the last several years breaking down the decade-long project of building a category-defining B2B company into more concrete steps — in hopes that we could create this resource that we feel is missing from the ecosystem.

利用我们自己的数据和来自500多项投资的观察，我们在过去几年中将打造定义品类的B2B公司这一长达十年的项目分解为更具体的步骤——希望我们能够创建出这个我们认为生态系统中缺失的资源。

To put it even more plainly: Despite all the talk about product-market fit, it didn't seem like anyone was meaningfully putting the pieces together to help B2B founders increase their odds of finding it. We wanted to change that.

说得更直白些：尽管大家都在谈论产品市场契合度，但似乎没有人真正将这些碎片整合起来，以帮助B2B创始人提高找到它的几率。我们想要改变这一点。

In addition to distilling lessons from decades of our own experience, we've layered on hundreds of hours of research and interviewed dozens of founders who've gotten further on this journey than most, from both within and outside of our community.

除了从我们数十年的经验中提炼教训外，我们还进行了数百小时的研究，并采访了数十位在这条路上走得比大多数人更远的创始人，他们来自我们社区内外。

As the culmination of that work, today we're proud to unveil Product-Market Fit Method, an intensive 14-week experience designed to help exceptional pre-seed founders build epic B2B SaaS companies.

作为这项工作的结晶，今天我们自豪地推出产品市场契合度方法——一个为期14周的强化体验，旨在帮助杰出的种子前创始人打造史诗般的B2B SaaS公司。

The program (which can be applied for here) consists of eight tactical sessions. We'll cover everything from validating your product insight and market, to approaching positioning, design partners, and product iteration, to learning the ropes of what we call 'dollar-driven discovery' and founder-led sales.

该项目（可在此处申请）包含八个战术课程。我们将涵盖从验证你的产品洞察和市场，到定位方法、设计合作伙伴和产品迭代，再到学习我们称之为美元驱动发现和创始人主导销售的方方面面。

While this is currently a highly-curated program with a limited number of spots, our tradition here at First Round has been to open-source everything we can to share what we've learned with the broader startup ecosystem.

虽然这是一个目前精心策划、名额有限的项目，但First Round的传统是尽可能开源一切，与更广泛的初创企业生态系统分享我们所学到的东西。

No commitment to applying. No email paywall (although of course, we'd highly recommend subscribing to The Review newsletter). No charge. And no equity required for the program either — our aim is to increase the odds that more founders find product-market fit, whether they're backed by First Round or not.

无需承诺申请。没有邮件付费墙（当然，我们强烈建议订阅The Review通讯）。不收费。该项目也不需要股权——我们的目标是增加更多创始人找到产品市场契合度的几率，无论他们是否由First Round支持。

The deck may seem stacked, but we're on a mission to rebalance the odds in the early founder's favor. We're firm believers that you don't have to go at it alone — and you don't have to make it all up as you go either.

局势可能看起来不利，但我们的使命是重新平衡对早期创始人有利的几率。我们坚信你不必独自前行——也不必边走边编造一切。

We've taken a systematic approach to defining what product-market fit is and identifying truly tactical steps for how to go about finding it.

我们采用系统化的方法来定义什么是产品市场契合度，并确定真正战术性的步骤来找到它。

PMF的层级

Levels of PMF

引入极致产品市场契合度

Introducing Extreme Product-Market Fit

Let's start with the obvious question: What is product-market fit? Here's our specific definition of what founders are aiming for — and why it's a bit different from what you may have seen before:

让我们从显而易见的问题开始：什么是产品市场契合度？以下是我们对创始人目标的明确定义——以及为什么它与你之前可能看到的有所不同：

极致产品市场契合度是一种状态：对满足关键需求的产品有广泛的需求——关键是——可以重复且高效地交付给每个客户。

Extreme product-market fit is a state of widespread demand for a product that satisfies a critical need and — crucially — can be delivered repeatably and efficiently to each customer.

As we've noted, the way most people have talked about product-market fit feels insufficient. More specifically, there are certain nuances that we feel haven't yet been adequately addressed:

正如我们所指出的，大多数人谈论产品市场契合度的方式感觉不够充分。更具体地说，我们认为某些细微差别尚未得到充分解决：

• Product-market fit progresses in levels. It's not a binary state where you have it and you're golden, or you don't and you're not on the path. But it's also not a vague spectrum or fuzzy sliding scale, where you can't be sure where you're at.

• 产品市场契合度是分层次推进的。它不是一个二元状态，要么你有它就很棒，要么没有就不在正确的道路上。但它也不是一个模糊的光谱或模糊的滑动尺度，你无法确定自己在哪里。

• There are a few dimensions of PMF that are in tension with each other, requiring deliberate tradeoffs at each level. There isn't just one element to emphasize above all else. And importantly, there are different times in a company's lifespan where founders need to intentionally prioritize one over another.

• PMF有几个维度相互之间存在张力，需要在每个层次上进行深思熟虑的权衡。不是只有一个元素需要凌驾于其他所有元素之上。重要的是，在公司生命周期的不同时间，创始人需要有意识地优先考虑一个维度而非另一个。

• There isn't nearly enough emphasis on repeatability. As you move your way up through the different levels of product-market fit, what you're really unlocking is repeatability — across demand generation, product development, customer satisfaction and unit economics.

• 对可重复性的重视远远不够。当你逐步提升产品市场契合度的不同层次时，你真正解锁的是可重复性——跨越需求生成、产品开发、客户满意度和单位经济学。

Repeatability is the holy grail on the hunt for product-market fit. Without any patterns, it's nearly impossible to generate early momentum and plot a course forward.

可重复性是寻找产品市场契合度的圣杯。没有任何模式，几乎不可能产生早期动力并规划前进路线。

That's why when we picture product-market fit, we see three distinct building blocks:

这就是为什么当我们想象产品市场契合度时，我们看到三个不同的构建模块：

Four Levels: Nascent, Developing, Strong, Extreme Product-Market Fit

四个层级：初生、发展、强劲、极致产品市场契合度

Three Dimensions: Satisfaction, Demand, Efficiency

三个维度：满意度、需求、效率

Levers: Change your persona, your problem, your promise, or your product (our version of the 4Ps) to make the work of finding, winning, serving and retaining your marginal customer easier.

杠杆：改变你的用户画像、你的问题、你的承诺或你的产品（我们版本的4P），使寻找、赢得、服务和留住你的边际客户的工作更容易。

产品市场契合度的四个层级

The 4 Levels of Product-Market Fit

We've identified four concrete levels of PMF, with what we're calling extreme product-market fit as the ultimate goal — what's required to build a generational company.

我们确定了PMF的四个具体层级，将我们所称的极致产品市场契合度作为最终目标——这是打造一家世代相传的公司所需要的。

Rather than a blanket 'you'll know it when you have it,' we've found that for enterprise companies there's a clear progression that companies go through that is fairly predictable. Many founders have walked this path, and based on what we've seen from the hundreds of early-stage startups we've backed, this journey to extreme product-market fit usually takes anywhere from two to six years.

我们发现，对于企业公司来说，有一个相当可预测的公司经历的明确进展，而不是笼统的当你有了它你就会知道。许多创始人走过这条路，根据我们从支持的数百家早期初创公司所看到的，通往极致产品市场契合度的旅程通常需要两到六年。

Our contention is that product market fit is not binary — and it's not something that you get to overnight, despite what all of those 'we just went viral' founding stories may have you believe.

我们的观点是，产品市场契合度不是二元的——也不是你一夜之间就能达到的，尽管所有那些我们就是走红了这样的创业故事可能让你相信这一点。

Once in a while, a company shoots up the ladder quickly, but that tends to be the exception. Some companies move up the progression over a longer period of time, while others skip ahead at different moments, leapfrogging from nascent to strong, for example.

偶尔，一家公司会迅速攀升，但这往往是例外。有些公司在更长的时间内逐步推进，而另一些公司在不同时刻跳跃前进，例如从初生跳到强劲。

We'll also note that the journey to PMF is a two-way ladder, meaning levels aren't permanently unlocked — it is possible to regress. To use another metaphor, think of it more as a dynamic equilibrium.

我们还要注意，通往PMF的旅程是一个双向阶梯，意味着层级不是永久解锁的——有可能倒退。用另一个比喻来说，把它想象成一个动态平衡。

We'll get deep into the details of what progressing through each level feels like further down, but staying at our current altitude, there's one high-level question you can ask here if you're unsure where your company stacks up: How hard is it to find and serve your next 'marginal' customer?

我们将深入探讨每个层级进展的感觉，但停留在当前高度，如果你不确定你的公司处于什么位置，有一个高层次的问题你可以问：找到并服务你的下一个边际客户有多难？

At Levels 3 and 4, for example, demand swells. Contrast that with Levels 1 and 2, where the search for the marginal customer feels more like a battle on several fronts — finding the right person, convincing them that your solution is what they need, converting them, and turning them into satisfied customers.

例如，在第3和第4层级，需求激增。相比之下，在第1和第2层级，寻找边际客户感觉更像是在多条战线上的战斗——找到合适的人，说服他们你的解决方案是他们需要的，转化他们，并让他们成为满意的客户。

产品市场契合度的三个维度

The 3 Dimensions of Product-Market Fit

We like to break that definition down even further into what we call the three dimensions of product-market fit:

我们喜欢将这个定义进一步分解为我们所说的产品市场契合度的三个维度：

• Satisfaction: How happy are customers with the product? Are you able to effectively retain customers? How much do people need the product? It may seem like splitting hairs, but we'll note that we've intentionally used 'need' and not 'love' here — there are many companies that have extreme PMF without customer love.

• 满意度：客户对产品有多满意？你能够有效地留住客户吗？人们有多需要这个产品？这可能看起来是在吹毛求疵，但我们要注意的是，我们在这里有意使用了需要而不是喜爱——有许多公司在没有客户喜爱的情况下拥有极致的PMF。

• Demand: Do you have the proverbial 'lines out the door'? How quickly and how much can you sell your product for?

• 需求：你有传说中的门外排长队吗？你能以多快的速度、多高的价格销售你的产品？

• Efficiency: Can the product grow repeatably and scale effectively? In our view, this is the critical piece that many definitions of PMF gloss over — even though it's one of the most important things that a company must achieve.

• 效率：产品能否可重复地增长并有效扩展？在我们看来，这是许多PMF定义所忽视的关键部分——尽管这是公司必须实现的最重要的东西之一。

Some may bristle at that last one. Most other definitions of product-market fit overtly focus on satisfaction and demand, implying that efficiency takes care of itself in the long run. But our belief is that while your business won't be efficient from day one — and may in fact be intentionally inefficient while you work to figure things out at Level 1 — there is tremendous value in building with an eye towards efficiency throughout your entire journey as a founder.

有些人可能对最后一点感到不满。大多数其他产品市场契合度的定义公开关注满意度和需求，暗示效率从长远来看会自行解决。但我们相信，虽然你的业务不会从第一天就高效——事实上，当你在Level 1努力解决问题时，可能是有意低效的——但在你作为创始人的整个旅程中，以效率为目标进行建设具有巨大的价值。

As a thought exercise, say you open up a stand on the side of the road where anyone who lines up and gives you $1 gets $100 in return. By classic definitions, product-market fit would be off the charts. You'd have lines out the door. Customers would be very satisfied. But it would be a mirage.

作为一个思维练习，假设你在路边开了一个摊位，任何排队给你1美元的人都会得到100美元的回报。按照经典定义，产品市场契合度将是超标的。你会门外排长队。客户会非常满意。但这将是一个海市蜃楼。

When you're starting a marathon, it's helpful to know what the final mile needs to look like — even if you don't alter your approach to mile 1.

当你开始一场马拉松时，知道最后一英里需要是什么样子是有帮助的——即使你不改变你对第一英里的方法。

First Round 4P：在通往产品市场契合度道路上摆脱困境的杠杆

The First Round 4Ps: Levers to Get Unstuck on Your Path to Product-Market Fit

As for how you can get unstuck and catapult your company to the next level, you can experiment with pulling these four levers at various points along your journey (our spin on the classic 4Ps framework):

至于如何摆脱困境并将你的公司推向下一层级，你可以在旅程的各个阶段尝试拉动这四个杠杆（我们对经典4P框架的诠释）：

• The persona: Who would benefit most from your insight? Do you have a person in a specific type of role or function who faces a set of challenges in mind? Example from Plaid: Consumers didn't need another budgeting app, but fintech apps needed the integrations the team had built on the back-end.

• 用户画像：谁最能从你的洞察中受益？你心中是否有一个特定类型角色或职能的人面临一系列挑战？来自Plaid的例子：消费者不需要另一个预算应用程序，但金融科技应用程序需要团队在后台构建的集成。

• The problem: Is this an urgent and important problem that your target persona has? It's the classic painkiller versus vitamin question. Example from Lattice: Stayed focused on people leaders, but went from building a solution for OKRs to solving performance management.

• 问题：这是你的目标用户画像面临的紧急且重要的问题吗？这是经典的止痛药与维生素问题。来自Lattice的例子：始终专注于人事领导者，但从为OKR构建解决方案转向解决绩效管理问题。

• The promise: Are those customers interested in what you'll do to address that need — your unique value proposition? Example from Ironclad: Repositioned from an AI legal assistant to a Contract Lifecycle Management platform.

• 承诺：这些客户是否对你将如何解决该需求感兴趣——你的独特价值主张？来自Ironclad的例子：从AI法律助手重新定位为合同生命周期管理平台。

• The product: Will the product that you're building actually deliver on this promise? Example from Alma: Pivoted during the pandemic from a community-based physical office concept for therapists, to giving providers a suite of digital tools.

• 产品：你正在构建的产品实际上会兑现这一承诺吗？来自Alma的例子：在疫情期间从为治疗师提供的社区型实体办公室概念转向为提供者提供一套数字工具。

极致产品市场契合度在现实世界中的样子

What Extreme Product-Market Fit Looks Like in the Real World

To bring this new concept of extreme product-market fit to life, let's walk through an example from Looker when it reached this Level 4 milestone as a Series C company with a team of 235 at the end of 2016 — after five years of hard work progressing through Levels 1-3.

为了让这个极致产品市场契合度的新概念生动起来，让我们看看Looker的例子，它在2016年底作为一家C轮公司，拥有235人的团队，经过五年努力从Level 1-3推进后，达到了这个Level 4里程碑。

Here's how Looker checked each box on our list of the three dimensions at this time:

以下是Looker当时如何在我们的三个维度清单上勾选每个框的：

• Demand: Went from 450 to nearly 800 customers in a year, growing revenue from $11.5M to $27M and increasing ACV to $57.7K.

• 需求：一年内从450个客户增加到近800个客户，收入从1150万美元增长到2700万美元，ACV增加到5.77万美元。

• Satisfaction: Extremely happy customers, with 141% NRR, a 18 month-streak of net negative churn and renewals higher than plan.

• 满意度：极度满意的客户，NRR达到141%，连续18个月实现净负流失，续约率高于计划。

• Efficient: The company had built an efficient model, with 77.6% gross margin.

• 效率：公司建立了高效的模型，毛利率达到77.6%。

Looker's path is a great example of each dimension coming together to contribute to extreme product-market fit. But just as there are success stories like these, in our experience, the absence of one or more of these dimensions is also the biggest reason that startups fail.

Looker的道路是一个很好的例子，说明每个维度如何结合起来促成极致产品市场契合度。但正如有这样的成功故事一样，根据我们的经验，缺少一个或多个这些维度也是初创公司失败的最大原因。

We believe that if you think about PMF in these terms, you'll be more likely to end up at strong or extreme levels of PMF than all the companies that didn't make it — in part because you and your co-founders will have a shared framework to more precisely diagnose where you might be falling short.

我们相信，如果你用这些术语来思考PMF，你比所有没有成功的公司更有可能最终达到强劲或极致的PMF水平——部分原因是你和你的联合创始人将有一个共享框架，可以更精确地诊断你可能在哪里做得不够。
day3_part2:
层级1：初生

Level 1: Nascent

At this stage, a founder's primary job is to find a problem worth solving for three to five customers. And by problem we mean an issue that is urgent and important for them, where solving it would be immensely valuable for their business.

在这个阶段，创始人的主要工作是为三到五个客户找到一个值得解决的问题。我们所说的问题是指对他们来说紧急且重要的问题，解决这个问题对他们的业务将有巨大的价值。

And while it's not the primary focus of Level 1, you should also be thinking about demand here, exploring whether you'll be able to find enough customers with that problem who will need your product.

虽然这不是Level 1的主要焦点，但你也应该在这里考虑需求，探索你是否能够找到足够多的有那个问题需要你的产品的客户。

In our experience, we've found that a fairly high percentage of startups get stuck at Level 1 — and stay stuck for six, 12, or even 18 months. That's when we've seen founders get bogged down in 'the grind.'

根据我们的经验，我们发现相当高比例的初创公司卡在Level 1——并且卡住六个月、12个月甚至18个月。那时我们看到创始人陷入苦战。

That's because the hallmark of this level is that it lacks repeatability — that's to be expected at Level 1. Finding the marginal customer is the opposite of easy.

这是因为这个层级的标志是缺乏可重复性——这在Level 1是意料之中的。找到边际客户绝非易事。

感觉如何

What it Feels Like

As we talked to sales-led B2B founders about how they navigated this first phase, unsurprisingly, several different paths emerged. Some grappled with finding the right product, others struggled to identify the target user, still others doubted the size of the market or even abandoned their original idea entirely.

当我们与销售主导的B2B创始人谈论他们如何度过第一阶段时， unsurprisingly，出现了几条不同的道路。有些人在寻找合适的产品上挣扎，另一些人在识别目标用户上遇到困难，还有一些人怀疑市场规模甚至完全放弃了他们原来的想法。

The common thread was wasted cycles before they stumbled upon those first few sparks of traction — which were more in the vein of 'aha moments' or 'this might just work' inklings, rather than hard metrics and the beginning of a hockey stick growth curve.

共同点是，在他们偶然发现最初的几个牵引力火花之前浪费了周期——这些更像是顿悟时刻或这可能行得通的预感，而不是硬指标和曲棍球棒增长曲线的开始。

Ironclad：通过实地拜访客户解锁更具体的产品

Ironclad: Unlocking a more specific product by visiting customers IRL

Ironclad is now a well-known digital contracting platform (last valued at $3.2B), but unpacking their journey to nascent product-market fit highlights just how much manual, non-scalable work you'll find at this level.

Ironclad现在是一个知名的数字合同平台（最近估值32亿美元），但解析他们通往初生产品市场契合度的旅程突显了在这个层级你会发现多少手动的、不可扩展的工作。

After serving as outside counsel for startups, co-founder Jason Boehmig started tinkering with automating parts of his own practice with coding that he had taught himself along the way.

在担任初创公司的外部法律顾问后，联合创始人Jason Boehmig开始尝试用他自学的编码来自动化他自己业务的部分工作。

现在我们正处于AI助手热潮中，回想起来这很疯狂，因为Ironclad的第一个版本是一个法律文件的自动化助手——但实际上是我在电子邮件地址的后端操作。

It's wild to think back to this now that we're in the AI assistant boom because the first version of Ironclad was an automated assistant for legal paperwork — but it was really me on the backend of an email address.

But it was spending time in person with their initial customers that helped narrow their product's focus.

但正是与最初客户面对面花时间帮助他们缩小了产品的焦点。

我记得我们飞了整个公司——当时只有四五个人——去博尔德见一个对我们有很多产品反馈的客户。

I remember that we flew out the whole company — which was four or five people at the time — to Boulder to see a customer who had a bunch of product feedback for us.

在Ironclad，早期的顿悟时刻来自观察我们的客户使用产品。这使我们能够选择一个我们可以追求的细分受众——法律运营——以及他们正在做的具体任务——常规交易。

At Ironclad, the early 'aha moments' came from watching our customers use the product. It enabled us to pick a niche audience that we could go after — legal operations — and the specific task they were doing — routine transactions.

Verkada：深入到极致，快速行动以打动客户

Verkada: Going unreasonably deep and moving fast to wow customers

Verkada has six different physical security product lines today, but the founders started back in 2016 with video security. Inspired by IoT devices in the consumer space, they realized the enterprise equivalent of applying software to video security was missing.

Verkada现在有六种不同的物理安全产品线，但创始人在2016年从视频安全开始。受到消费领域物联网设备的启发，他们意识到将软件应用于视频安全的企业级等效产品是缺失的。

When co-founder Filip Kaliszan thinks back to the nascent stage of their journey to PMF, the team's speed and willingness to get in the field stand out.

当联合创始人Filip Kaliszan回想起他们通往PMF旅程的初生阶段时，团队的速度和进入现场的意愿脱颖而出。

第一个版本看起来很滑稽。它是一个基于树莓派的相机。我们只是用不同的零件拼凑在一起——供应链是Amazon.com。我们大概建了100个这样的小相机，然后把它们送给了我们认识的朋友和企业。

The very first version looked very funny. It was a Raspberry Pi based camera. We just hacked it together from different parts — the supply chain was Amazon.com. We built maybe 100 of these little cameras and we gave them to friends and businesses we knew.

对于最初的几位客户，我们只是尽一切努力让客户尽快部署，然后与这位客户保持密切联系，了解什么对他们有效，什么无效。

For the first few customers, we just did whatever it took to get the customer deployed as fast as possible and then stayed close to that customer to learn what was working for them and what wasn't.

当你有任何早期需求拉动的迹象时，你必须跟随它，并尽一切努力让它发生。

When you have any signs of early demand pull, you have to follow it and work as hard as it takes to just make it happen.

Plaid：在未经验证的市场中转型并建立信念

Plaid: Pivoting and building conviction in an unproven market

Plaid is now known as the pioneer of the 'invisible plumbing' fintech infrastructure that powers everything from paying your friends back on Venmo, to making an investment on Robinhood, to buying Bitcoin on Coinbase. But the product actually started out as a consumer budgeting app.

Plaid现在被称为隐形管道金融科技基础设施的先驱，它支撑着从在Venmo上还钱给朋友，到在Robinhood上投资，再到在Coinbase上购买比特币的一切。但这个产品实际上最初是一个消费者预算应用程序。

消费者的反应让我们很快意识到我们不擅长构建预算应用程序。我们尝试了一段时间。我们构建了六个不同版本的预算管理，分析你的支出类型的应用程序，但它们从未真正获得任何吸引力。

The response of consumers was such that we pretty quickly realized we weren't going to be any good at building budgeting apps. We tried for a while. We built six different versions of budgeting management, analyzing your spend types of applications and they didn't really ever get any traction.

有一天，一位Venmo早期工程师的朋友来找我们说，嘿，你们的消费产品不是很好，但我想授权你们构建的后端，你们与银行集成以将数据获取到应用程序中的方式。

One day, a friend who was one of the early engineers at Venmo came to us and said, 'Hey, your consumer products are not so good, but I'd like to license the backend that you've built, the way that you've integrated with the banks in order to get the data into your app.'

当我回想起Plaid产品市场契合度旅程的最早阶段时，挑战不是找到人们会购买的产品。而是围绕这个产品创建一个市场，我们相信这对未来非常重要，但尚未得到验证。

When I think back to the earliest stage of Plaid's product-market fit journey, the challenge wasn't finding a product that people would buy. It was creating a market around the product that we believed would be very important for the future, but was yet to be proven.

看起来如何：初生需求、满意度、效率案例研究

What it Looks Like: A Case Study on Nascent Demand, Satisfaction, Efficiency in Action

To return to the very beginning of our example from Looker's actual trajectory, the data platform startup spent roughly from August 2011 to March 2013 in the nascent stage.

回到我们Looker实际轨迹示例的最开始，这个数据平台初创公司大约在2011年8月至2013年3月期间处于初生阶段。

我们等了将近一年才筹集资金，直到我们知道这是一家风险企业——并非每个初创公司都是，你不想被锁定。我们与客户和收入一起努力前进。

We waited almost a year to raise money, until we knew it was a venture business — not every startup is, and you don't want to get locked in. We were cranking along with customers and revenue.

Here's how the business looked at the nascent stage: With that early glimmer of traction, Looker raised a $2 million seed round from First Round and PivotNorth in August 2012. By the end of Q1 of 2013, the 9-person team was at $357K in ARR with 15 customers.

以下是业务在初生阶段的样子：凭借早期的牵引力微光，Looker于2012年8月从First Round和PivotNorth筹集了200万美元的种子轮。到2013年第一季度末，这个9人团队的ARR达到35.7万美元，拥有15个客户。

But to show an even more detailed snapshot of what 'good' looks like at this level, we present a case study on our first fictitious startup: Ledgerly, a platform that uses LLMs to help accounting teams.

但为了展示在这个层级好是什么样子的更详细的快照，我们介绍了第一个虚构初创公司的案例研究：Ledgerly，一个使用LLM帮助会计团队的平台。

The Ledgerly team is pretty new, with just five full-time employees. They raised a $1.2 million pre-seed round earlier this year and they don't even know what their burn multiple is — but that's appropriate, given how early they are.

Ledgerly团队很新，只有五名全职员工。他们今年早些时候筹集了120万美元的pre-seed轮，他们甚至不知道自己的燃烧倍数是多少——但考虑到他们处于多么早期的阶段，这是合适的。

Let's walk through each of the dimensions of product-market fit:

让我们逐一了解产品市场契合度的各个维度：

• Demand: They only have four customers, all of which they acquired through warm intros from their personal networks or from investors. They don't have an outbound sales machine yet.

• 需求：他们只有四个客户，都是通过他们个人网络或投资者的热情介绍获得的。他们还没有外呼销售机器。

• Satisfaction: All of their customers are starting to use the product regularly, at least once a week. Two of them have onboarded multiple users internally.

• 满意度：他们所有的客户都开始定期使用产品，至少每周一次。其中两个已经在内部引入了多个用户。

• Efficiency: This is the area the team has paid the least attention to, which is appropriate given their stage. Customer onboarding feels too lengthy (currently at eight weeks).

• 效率：这是团队关注最少的领域，考虑到他们的阶段，这是合适的。客户入职感觉太长（目前为八周）。

基准指标

Benchmarks

While the above hopefully gives you a sense of what nascent product-market fit feels like, we've also collected these benchmarks that indicate your company is at Level 1 so you can more closely compare your progress.

虽然上述内容 hopefully 让你对初生产品市场契合度的感觉有了了解，但我们也收集了这些基准指标，表明你的公司处于Level 1，以便你可以更密切地比较你的进展。

• Your team is less than 10 people.

• 你的团队少于10人。

• You're at the pre-seed or seed-stage.

• 你处于pre-seed或种子阶段。

• Demand source is mostly friends and network, some cold outreach.

• 需求来源主要是朋友和网络，一些冷 outreach。

• Sales conversions: 1 out of every 10-20 warm intros converts to a customer.

• 销售转化率：每10-20个热情介绍中有1个转化为客户。

• 3-5 customers.

• 3-5个客户。

• $0-$500K ARR.

• 0-50万美元ARR。

• No renewals yet (too early). No regretted churn (some non-regretted).

• 还没有续约（太早）。没有遗憾流失（一些非遗憾流失）。

最重要的是什么

What Matters Most

To reach Level 2, founders need to focus on satisfaction most. In the case of our fictitious startup, Ledgerly, in order to move the needle on satisfaction, their goal is to increase daily use and number of users at each company.

要达到Level 2，创始人最需要关注满意度。对于我们虚构的初创公司Ledgerly来说，为了提高满意度，他们的目标是增加每个公司的日常使用量和用户数量。

With satisfaction front and center, demand is second at this stage. While you don't need heaps of customers, you do need an inkling that if you make this small set of customers happy, eventually there will be that proverbial line out the door.

以满意度为首要，需求在这个阶段排在第二位。虽然你不需要大量客户，但你确实需要一种预感，即如果你让这一小群客户满意，最终会有传说中的门外排长队。

Efficiency comes in last at this stage. We'd recommend that you don't spend a ton of time thinking about it at Level 1.

效率在这个阶段排在最后。我们建议你在Level 1不要花太多时间思考它。

早期与客户保持密切联系是培养这种满意度的关键。但这里有一个重要的区别。如果你没有给他们价值，他们不想靠近你。

Sticking close to your customers early on is key to cultivating that satisfaction. But there's an important distinction here. If you're not giving them value, they don't want to be near you.

对于Looker来说，寻找产品市场契合度是关于弄清楚如何交付价值。对于我们的前四个客户，我有不同的方式来交付我正在构建的东西，从纯咨询到独立产品。

For Looker, the search for product-market fit was about figuring out how to deliver the value. For our first four customers, I had different ways of delivering what I was building, from pure consulting to a standalone product.

我毫不犹豫地将不成比例的资源投入到这里。在SaaS中，专注于让客户成功是一种保留策略，而不是成本中心。

I had no qualms about sinking a disproportionate amount of resources here. In SaaS, focusing on making your customer successful is a retention strategy, not a cost center.

需要注意的警告信号

Yellow Flags to Watch Out for

It's hard to tell in the moment if you're mistaking a more tepid response for early traction, or if an extra dose of founder grit will unlock the breakthrough your business needs.

很难判断你是否将更冷淡的反应误认为是早期牵引力，或者额外的创始人毅力是否会解锁你的业务所需的突破。

许多早期初创公司面临的最大挑战是他们在客户满意度方面对自己撒谎。在这个阶段，一个常见的陷阱是有喜欢但不爱或不需要你的产品的客户。

The biggest challenge for a lot of early-stage startups is that they lie to themselves about customer satisfaction. It's a common trap at this stage to have customers who like but don't love or need your product.

我在Persona内部经常使用关系类比：知道某人喜欢但不爱某样东西是非常非常困难的，但如果你曾经处于朋友区，你就知道那个答案。

I use the relationship analogy a lot internally at Persona: knowing when someone likes but doesn't love something is very, very difficult but if you've ever been in the friend zone, you know that answer.

Here are the yellow flags we recommend watching out for:

以下是我们建议注意的警告信号：

• Customers respond that if your product disappeared tomorrow, they wouldn't be disappointed.

• 客户回应说，如果你的产品明天消失，他们不会失望。

• Usage is low and not growing over a period of six months.

• 使用量低，并且在六个月的时间内没有增长。

• You have a handful of happy customers, but the most important feature is actually different for each of them.

• 你有少数满意的客户，但对他们来说最重要的功能实际上是不同的。

• The sales cycles take too long, and if your champion quits the company, then the deal just falls apart.

• 销售周期太长，如果你的支持者离开公司，交易就会崩溃。

如果你卡住了，你摆脱初生阶段的唯一方法是大的改变，而不是小的改变。很多人在初生阶段徘徊，多年来只做小的改变，这实际上是在浪费每个人的时间和资源。

If you're stuck, your only way out of the nascent stage is big changes, not small changes. A lot of people hang out in nascent and make small changes for years, and that's just a literal waste of everybody's time and resources.

通常情况下，如果你正在做的事情没有得到很好的牵引力，你可能不是离10%的调整——你可能离200%的调整更远。

More often than not, if you're working on something that is not getting great traction, you're probably not a 10% adjustment away — you're probably a 200% adjustment away.

层级2：发展中

Level 2: Developing

By Level 2, you've ticked through some of the classic milestones, such as building something 20 or so customers need, or getting to your first $1M in ARR. And while you haven't totally figured it all out, there's also much more repeatability in your customers' needs, the messaging that resonates, and the product solution you're delivering.

到了Level 2，你已经完成了一些经典的里程碑，比如构建大约20个客户需要的东西，或者达到你的第一个100万美元ARR。虽然你还没有完全弄清楚一切，但你的客户需求、引起共鸣的信息传递以及你交付的产品解决方案都有了更多的可重复性。

Developing PMF is when you're starting to see glimmering signs that the marginal customer is more within reach — but it by no means feels easy yet. This is one of the reasons why Level 2 is one of the two levels that the majority of companies get stuck on.

发展PMF是指你开始看到边际客户更容易触及的微光迹象——但这绝不意味着感觉容易。这是Level 2是大多数公司卡住的两个层级之一的原因之一。

感觉如何

What it Feels Like

当时市场拉动是什么样子非常明显。有些人在从未接触过真实产品的情况下就向我们支付年度预付合同，只是基于我们的设计模型——这与我们在OKR方面的经历完全不同。

It was very obvious what market pull looked like then. We had people paying us annual upfront contracts without ever touching a real product, just on our design mocks — which couldn't be more different than the experience we'd been having with OKRs.

有潜在客户从各地自然涌来。人们在向他们的朋友推荐我们。人们会说，我两周后要启动这个绩效周期，我需要这个功能。你们还没有。我们明天能安排时间讨论一下，以便我们帮助你们构建它吗？

There were leads coming organically from all over the place. People were telling their friends about us. People would say, 'I'm kicking off this performance cycle in two weeks and I need this feature. You don't have it yet. Can we schedule time tomorrow to talk it through so we can help you build it?'

一旦你有了可行的东西，即使面对一个相当不成熟、不完整、半损坏的产品，它也会奏效，而我们在这个阶段当然有这样的产品。

Once you've got something that's going to work, it's going to work even in the face of a pretty undeveloped, incomplete, semi-broken product, which we certainly had at this stage.

看起来如何：发展中的需求、满意度、效率案例研究

What it Looks Like: A Case Study on Developing Demand, Satisfaction, Efficiency in Action

Let's rewind the clock back to when Looker was at Level 2, roughly from March 2013 to Nov 2014. The startup emerged from stealth in March 2013 with a 34-person team and 20 customers, many of which were also in the First Round community.

让我们把时间倒回到Looker处于Level 2的时候，大约从2013年3月到2014年11月。这家初创公司于2013年3月从隐身模式中脱颖而出，拥有34人的团队和20个客户，其中许多也在First Round社区中。

By August 2013, they'd made enough progress to raise a $16M Series A round. By November 2013, they'd hit the coveted $1M in ARR milestone and grown to over 40 customers.

到2013年8月，他们已经取得了足够的进展，筹集了1600万美元的A轮融资。到2013年11月，他们达到了令人垂涎的100万美元ARR里程碑，并增长到40多个客户。

As for our example company at this level, meet Hire Hero, a fictional interviewing platform that uses AI to help teams hire better. They have got 18 full-time employees. They recently raised a $10M Series A round, and they've got a burn multiple of 4.2x.

对于我们在这个层级的示例公司，让我们认识Hire Hero，一个虚构的面试平台，使用AI帮助团队更好地招聘。他们有18名全职员工。他们最近筹集了1000万美元的A轮融资，他们的燃烧倍数是4.2倍。

Let's walk through each of the dimensions of product-market fit:

让我们逐一了解产品市场契合度的各个维度：

• Demand: They're at $1.2M in ARR and they have 22 customers. Their average sales cycle is 32 days, but that's an improvement over 47 days.

• 需求：他们的ARR为120万美元，有22个客户。他们的平均销售周期是32天，但比47天有所改善。

• Satisfaction: This team has been around long enough to actually track renewals, and four of their customers have renewed. Weekly active usage is going up.

• 满意度：这个团队已经存在足够长的时间来实际跟踪续约，他们的四个客户已经续约。每周活跃使用量正在上升。

• Efficiency: At this stage, it's still not a focus, but at least they know they're at 65% gross margin. Their onboarding has gotten 15% faster.

• 效率：在这个阶段，它仍然不是重点，但至少他们知道他们的毛利率是65%。他们的入职速度提高了15%。

基准指标

Benchmarks

• Team size: Up to 20 people.

• 团队规模：最多20人。

• You're either a Seed or Series A company.

• 你是种子轮或A轮公司。

• Demand source: Early signs of a scalable channel that doesn't depend on warm intros.

• 需求来源：可扩展渠道的早期迹象，不依赖于热情介绍。

• 5-25 customers.

• 5-25个客户。

• $500K to $5M ARR.

• 50万到500万美元ARR。

• Some renewals, 10-20% regretted churn, NRR at least 100%.

• 一些续约，10-20%的遗憾流失，NRR至少100%。

最重要的是什么

What Matters Most

To level up, your priority needs to shift to demand. Here's why: We've seen true grind-it-out founders muscle their way to 20 customers through sheer willpower and effort. But you can't will your way to 100 or 200 customers — the satisfaction and demand for the product are starting to do the work for you.

要升级，你的优先级需要转向需求。原因如下：我们看到真正的苦干型创始人通过纯粹的意志力和努力强行获得20个客户。但你无法凭意志力获得100或200个客户——产品的满意度和需求开始为你工作。

口碑很棒，但在B2B中，你还需要扩展获取客户的方式。你需要比你想象的更早开始这项工作。

Word of mouth is wonderful, but in B2B you also need to scale ways you're acquiring customers. You need to start on that work earlier than you think.

需要注意的警告信号

Yellow Flags to Watch Out for

• Current customers are happy, but you're having trouble opening the floodgates.

• 当前客户很满意，但你在打开闸门方面遇到困难。

• Regretted churn is greater than 20%.

• 遗憾流失大于20%。

• Starting to get concerned about your burn multiple.

• 开始担心你的燃烧倍数。

• Sales cycle is taking too long and you lose deals late in the funnel.

• 销售周期太长，你在漏斗后期失去交易。

本篇为上篇内容（框架介绍 + 层级1：初生 + 层级2：发展中），下篇将在 Day4 继续。
day4_part1:
层级3：强劲

Level 3: Strong

In our experience, the majority of startups never get to this level. If you get to strong product-market fit, your company has the potential to be very valuable.

根据我们的经验，大多数初创公司从未达到这个层级。如果你达到了强劲的产品市场契合度，你的公司有可能非常有价值。

The big difference between Level 2 and Level 3 is that the demand floodgates have opened. There's even more repeatability. Demand is coming inbound. People have heard of you. The company feels like it's humming.

Level 2和Level 3之间的最大区别是需求闸门已经打开。有更多的可重复性。需求正在涌入。人们听说过你。公司感觉正在嗡嗡作响。

感觉如何

What it Feels Like

我们和某人交谈过，他说找到产品市场契合度是如此直观，你立即就能感觉到——就像间歇泉爆发一样。老实说，我们在头几年从未有过这种感觉。

We talked to someone who said that finding product-market fit was so visceral that you immediately felt it — like a geyser exploding. And we honestly never felt that in the first couple years.

在Retool，我们获得的每一个早期客户，无论是第四个还是第十四个，都感觉像是我们将要找到的最后一个客户。感觉更像是在滚石上山。

At Retool every early customer we got, whether that was number four or number 14, felt like the last customer we were ever going to find. It felt more like rolling the stone uphill.

最大的转变是获取潜在客户的容易程度。我记得当时想，我甚至不知道这些潜在客户是从哪里来的。而且每个月都有更多的潜在客户出现。

The biggest shift was in the ease of getting leads. I remember thinking, 'I don't even know where these leads are coming from.' And more just kept showing up every month.

在2018年第一年的销售之后，接下来的两年是疯狂的。我们几乎跟不上生产。我们必须扩展所有系统。

After our first year of sales in 2018, those next two years were crazy. We were barely keeping up with production. We had to scale all the systems.

看起来如何：强劲需求、满意度、效率案例研究

What it Looks Like: A Case Study on Strong Demand, Satisfaction, Efficiency in Action

Let's start again by tracing back to when Looker fit into Level 3, roughly from November 2014 to December 2015. By November 2014, the team had passed the $5M ARR mark.

让我们再次追溯到Looker符合Level 3的时候，大约从2014年11月到2015年12月。到2014年11月，团队已经超过了500万美元ARR的门槛。

In February 2015, the company raised a $30M Series B round, noting they'd grown to 110 employees, and increased revenue by 400%.

2015年2月，该公司筹集了3000万美元的B轮融资，指出他们已经增长到110名员工，收入增长了400%。

At this point, Looker had 250 customers, adding new customers such as Uber, Instacart, Plaid, and Etsy.

此时，Looker拥有250个客户，新增了Uber、Instacart、Plaid和Etsy等客户。

For our Level 3 case study, let's introduce GuardDog, a fictional incident management tool to help security teams seamlessly identify and address security issues.

对于我们的Level 3案例研究，让我们介绍GuardDog，一个虚构的事件管理工具，帮助安全团队无缝识别和解决安全问题。

They have 64 full-time employees and are planning to raise a $25M Series B. Their burn multiple is now 2.1, which is getting respectable.

他们有64名全职员工，正计划筹集2500万美元的B轮融资。他们的燃烧倍数现在是2.1，这正在变得令人尊敬。

Let's walk through each of the dimensions of product-market fit:

让我们逐一了解产品市场契合度的各个维度：

• Demand: GuardDog has 88 customers. With $10M in ARR and 14% sales conversion, they've got inbound and outbound channels that are working.

• 需求：GuardDog有88个客户。ARR为1000万美元，销售转化率为14%，他们有有效的inbound和outbound渠道。

• Satisfaction: They're at 108% net revenue retention, and 8% regretted churn per year. Quarterly user engagement has gone from 5.5 average users per customer up to nine.

• 满意度：他们的净收入保留率为108%，每年遗憾流失率为8%。季度用户参与度从每个客户平均5.5个用户上升到9个。

• Efficiency: GuardDog is getting into the respectable zone here. The team did a ton of work both on the infrastructure side, and the services side to improve to a 71% gross margin.

• 效率：GuardDog正在进入令人尊敬的区域。团队在基础设施方面和服务方面做了大量工作，将毛利率提高到71%。

基准指标

Benchmarks

• Team size: 30-100 people.

• 团队规模：30-100人。

• Stage: Series A/B/C company.

• 阶段：A/B/C轮公司。

• Demand source: 1+ scalable channels (you've cracked marketing and sales).

• 需求来源：1个以上可扩展渠道（你已经破解了营销和销售）。

• 25-100 customers.

• 25-100个客户。

• $5-25M ARR.

• 500万到2500万美元ARR。

• >110% NRR。

• >110% NRR.

• Burn multiple in 1-3X range.

• 燃烧倍数在1-3倍范围内。

最重要的是什么

What Matters Most

To reach the next level, now is the time that you have to get serious about efficient economics. CAC payback, LTV to CAC ratio — these are all the metrics that really start to matter.

要达到下一层级，现在是你必须认真对待高效经济的时候了。CAC回收期、LTV与CAC比率——这些都是真正开始重要的指标。

The difference between strong and extreme product-market fit is that you have to successfully stay on that growth curve and make the whole operation more efficient at the same time. It's a highwire act.

强劲和极致产品市场契合度之间的区别在于，你必须成功地保持在这条增长曲线上，同时使整个运营更高效。这是一种走钢丝的行为。

需要注意的警告信号

Yellow Flags to Watch Out for

• You've got a little bit of a leaky bucket: NRR below 90%, regretted churn greater than 10%.

• 你的桶有点漏：NRR低于90%，遗憾流失大于10%。

• Referrals and word of mouth have started to plateau.

• 推荐和口碑已经开始趋于平稳。

• Growth is slowing down. You grew 3X each of the prior two years, but you're struggling to hit 2X this year.

• 增长正在放缓。你在前两年每年增长3倍，但今年你正在努力达到2倍。

在设定季度目标时，不要偏离你的模型太远。在可以更快的地方走得更快，但不要采取让团队感觉像要溺水的粗暴方法。

When it comes to setting your targets for the quarter, don't stray too far from your model. Go faster where you can go faster, but don't take a blunt force approach that makes your team feel like they're drowning.
day4_part2:
层级4：极致

Level 4: Extreme

Finally, Level 4: extreme product-market fit, which should be the goal of every startup.

最后，Level 4：极致产品市场契合度，这应该是每个初创公司的目标。

If you're at this stage, repeatability shines through in your ability to win over customers that are in your crisply defined target persona — it doesn't mean you have a 100% conversion rate, but there's tremendous consistency.

如果你处于这个阶段，可重复性在你赢得清晰定义的目标用户画像客户的能力中闪耀——这并不意味着你有100%的转化率，但有巨大的consistency。

This is also often where a company's brand often begins to inflect. With more widespread recognition, you may be on the cusp of becoming the 'Kleenex' of your category.

这也是公司品牌通常开始变化的阶段。随着更广泛的认可，你可能正处于成为你品类的舒洁的边缘。

感觉如何

What it Feels Like

我们听说过其他过早尝试的公司的恐怖故事。但我们得到了很好的建议，在你感觉像是在半速骑行之前，在你作为CEO对核心市场取得的所有进展感到满意之前，你不应该扩张。

We'd heard horror stories from other companies that tried too early. But we got good advice that you shouldn't expand until you feel like you're pedaling at half speed, until you as the CEO are comfortable with all the progress that is being made in your core market.

在公司建设的早期，你在骑自行车上坡，尽可能用力地踩踏板。当到了扩大你的TAM的时候，你在寻找那种感觉，你不是在下坡，而是在平坦的道路上，没有那么多阻力。

Earlier in company building, you're going uphill on a bike and pedaling as hard as you can. When it comes time to expand your TAM, you're looking for that feeling where you're not going downhill, but you're on a flat road and there isn't as much resistance.

看起来如何：极致需求、满意度、效率案例研究

What it Looks Like: A Case Study on Extreme Demand, Satisfaction, Efficiency in Action

Our example company for this level is called Metriq, a fictional intelligence platform that delivers self-service analytics and reports so teams can make decisions faster.

我们这个层级的示例公司叫做Metriq，一个虚构的智能平台，提供自助分析和服务，以便团队可以更快地做出决策。

They're at 186 employees and they just raised a $75 million Series C. Their burn multiple is looking very good at .87 — last month they brought in $2M of net-new ARR, and only burned $1.73M.

他们有186名员工，刚刚筹集了7500万美元的C轮融资。他们的燃烧倍数看起来非常好，为0.87——上个月他们带来了200万美元的净新增ARR，只消耗了173万美元。

Let's walk through each of the dimensions of product-market fit:

让我们逐一了解产品市场契合度的各个维度：

• Demand: They've got 262 customers. They're almost at $30M in ARR. Sales conversion is 24%. Their ACV is $112K. They grew ARR 3x over the prior 12 months.

• 需求：他们有262个客户。他们的ARR接近3000万美元。销售转化率为24%。他们的ACV是11.2万美元。他们在过去12个月里ARR增长了3倍。

• Satisfaction: Their customers are using the platform regularly and without prompting — the product is that important to them. They're at 123% NRR, and their regretted churn is only 6%.

• 满意度：他们的客户定期使用平台，无需提示——产品对他们如此重要。他们的NRR为123%，遗憾流失仅为6%。

• Efficiency: The sales, onboarding, and customer success teams are running smoothly. They've got repeatable playbooks. Gross margin is up to 86% and they're at an 11 month CAC payback.

• 效率：销售、入职和客户成功团队运行顺利。他们有可重复的操作手册。毛利率高达86%，CAC回收期为11个月。

基准指标

Benchmarks

• Team size: 100+ people.

• 团队规模：100多人。

• Company is at Series C or beyond.

• 公司处于C轮或更后期。

• Demand Source: 2+ scalable demand channels.

• 需求来源：2个以上可扩展需求渠道。

• 100+ customers.

• 100多个客户。

• $25M+ ARR.

• 2500万美元以上ARR。

• >120% NRR。

• >120% NRR.

• Burn multiple 0-1X.

• 燃烧倍数0-1倍。

最重要的是什么

What Matters Most

Whether it's Salesforce, Stripe, or Datadog, all the legendary enterprise companies have managed to enter multiple markets with multiple products and reach extreme PMF time and time again in new customer segments.

无论是Salesforce、Stripe还是Datadog，所有传奇的企业公司都成功地用多个产品进入多个市场，并在新的客户细分市场中一次又一次地达到极致PMF。

If you want your company to compound value, you're not done with growth in year six or seven, or just because you've reached a certain size. Growing over a long period of time is actually the act of finding extreme PMF over and over again.

如果你想让你的公司复利增值，你在第六年或第七年还没有完成增长，或者仅仅因为你达到了一定的规模。长期增长实际上是一遍又一遍地寻找极致PMF的行为。

我们在Atlassian的第二年创建了我们的第二个产品Confluence，这真的非常不寻常——尤其是当你有一个像Jira这样增长非常好的突破性产品时。

We created our second product, Confluence, in Atlassian's second year, which is really unusual — especially when you've got a breakout product like Jira that's growing really nicely.

在Atlassian非常早期的阶段，我们就开始训练我们的大脑思考公司随着规模扩大需要做的所有这些事情。

At a really early age at Atlassian, we began to wire our brain to think about all of those things that companies need to do as they get bigger.

Once you've reached extreme product-market fit and are ready to attempt to do it all over again, you can effectively expand your TAM — which is how you compound revenue — by remixing one (and eventually all) of three components: your product, your market or your buyer.

一旦你达到了极致产品市场契合度并准备再次尝试，你可以通过重新组合三个组件中的一个（最终是全部）来有效扩展你的TAM——这就是你如何复利收入的方式：你的产品、你的市场或你的买家。

That could look like: Creating new product use cases by adding new features and functionality. Creating a net-new product that you sell in the same market to the same buyer. Taking the same product and expanding into a different market. Selling the same product in the same market to a different buyer.

这可能看起来像是：通过添加新功能和功能创建新的产品用例。创建一个全新的产品，你在同一市场卖给同一个买家。采用相同的产品并扩展到不同的市场。在同一市场向不同的买家销售相同的产品。

It's like you're assembling a layer cake over many many years. You're adding on new features, new products, new customers, new market segments — trying to stack your way to a multi-billion dollar valuation without knocking the whole thing over.

就像你在很多很多年里组装一个千层蛋糕。你在添加新功能、新产品、新客户、新市场细分——试图在不把整个东西撞倒的情况下，堆叠出一条通往数十亿美元估值的道路。

尽管我现在经常开玩笑说Vanta的早期日子以及我们对所有不同的初创想法感到多么困惑，但事实是那段时期真的很艰难。

As much as I now joke about Vanta's early days and how confused we were with all of our different startup ideas, the truth is that period is really rough.

从外部看，你可以将创始人的生活浪漫化，并想，世界是你的牡蛎。你可以构建任何你想要的东西。这些都是真的，但在日常中，更像是，我在做什么？有人在乎吗？会有人在乎吗？

From the outside, you can romanticize a founder's life and think, 'The world's your oyster. You can build whatever you want.' And that's all true, but in the day-to-day, it's more like, 'What am I doing? Does anyone care? Will anyone ever care?'
